preamble: The shift from the mid-back rounded vowel [o] to the high-front rounded vowel [y] involves both Raising and Fronting. This is most famously seen in the early stages of Germanic Umlaut (i-mutation), where a back vowel was both fronted and raised under the influence of a following high front sound.

examples.note: Old English long /o/ underwent i-mutation to /y/ in plural forms, later unrounding to /i/.
In Scots and Northern English, long /o/ fronted to /y/ or /ø/ rather than raising to /u/ as in Southern English.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction.
Hock, H. H. (1991). Principles of Historical Linguistics.
Ladefoged, P. (2001). A Course in Phonetics.
